Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

General Safety
Q: What is the emergency stop (E-stop) procedure?
A: Press the nearest red mushroom-head E-stop button. This immediately de-energises the machine.
Shout 'E-STOP ACTIVATED' to alert nearby workers. Do NOT restart the machine after an E-stop
without first investigating and resolving the cause, and obtaining Shift Supervisor authorisation. Notify
the Shift Supervisor of every E-stop event.
Q: I can smell strong solvent but no alarm has triggered. What should I do?
A: Evacuate the area immediately and activate the nearest Andon. Do not wait for an alarm. Notify the
Shift Supervisor verbally. This may indicate a LEV failure or solvent spill before the sensor has detected
it. Re-entry only with Supervisor and EHS authorisation. Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §3, Step 3
— when in doubt, stop.
Q: If a quality hold is raised, who do I notify and how quickly?
A: Notify your Shift Supervisor and QA Lead within 15 minutes of raising the hold. Use MES hold
notification plus a verbal communication. Do not release a quality hold without QA Lead sign-off.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §4.

results.question: What is the maximum mercury content allowed in batteries until 2025?
results.original_answer: 0.0005% (5 ppm) by weight
results.llm_answer: Until 2025, batteries can contain up to 0.0005% mercury by weight.
results.context:
9. Appendix: Prohibitions and Labelling Requirements:
• Prohibition on Heavy Metal Content in Batteries:
• Batteries containing up to 0.0005% (5 ppm) of mercury by weight may only be placed until
2025.
• Batteries containing up to 0.002% (2000 ppm) of cadmium by weight may only be placed.
• The prohibition on mercury content shall not apply to button zinc silver oxide batteries with
mercury content < 2% and button zinc air batteries with mercury content < 2% by weight.
• The prohibition on cadmium content shall not apply to portable batteries intended for use in
emergency and alarm systems, including emergency lighting, and medical equipment.
**************
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)
Battery Waste Management
1.Purpose: This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is meticulously designed to ensure effective battery
waste management in strict compliance with the Battery Waste Management Rules of 2022. The SOP
applies comprehensively to all units of THDCIL and encompasses the responsibilities and procedures
concerning the collection, segregation, transportation, and disposal of waste batteries. It also outlines the
prohibitions on heavy metal content in batteries.
2. Scope: This SOP is applicable comprehensively to all Projects/Units/Offices of THDCIL.
3. Definitions:
• Battery: Refers to new or refurbished cells and/or batteries and/or their components, including
accumulators, that generate electrical energy through the direct conversion of chemical energy. This
includes disposable primary and/or secondary batteries.
• Waste Battery: Encompasses various types of batteries, including used and/or end-of-life batteries,
pre-consumer off-spec batteries, batteries with expired usage dates, and batteries discarded by users.
4. Responsibility of Waste Generator (i.e. THDCIL):
It is the duty of consumer units/offices within THDCIL to:
• Discard waste batteries separately from other waste streams, especially mixed waste and domestic
waste.
• Ensure that waste batteries are disposed of in an environmentally friendly manner by giving them
to entities engaged in the collection, refurbishment, or recycling of batteries.
• Storage: THDCIL Projects/Units/Offices must store waste batteries in designated containers or
storage areas to prevent leakage and contamination. Separate storage facilities must be provided for
different types of batteries (e.g., primary and secondary batteries) and must comply with safety
regulations.
• Segregation: Waste batteries should be segregated based on their type, chemistry, and heavy metal
content. Separate containers labelled for different battery types (e.g., mercury-containing batteries)
should be used for proper identification.
• Transportation: Transportation of waste batteries should be done using specially designed and
covered transport systems to prevent foul odours, spillage, and environmental contamination.
• Disposal: Waste batteries should be disposed of through authorized entities engaged in battery
collection, refurbishment, or recycling. Ensure compliance with environmental regulations and
guidelines during disposal.

Electrode Slurry Mixing — Cathode & Anode
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1
Process Area Electrode Mixing Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 3
1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
4. Materials & Equipment
Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification
Active Material NMC811 powder Artificial Graphite Per BOM – batch card
Binder PVDF (Kynar 761) CMC + SBR Battery grade
Solvent NMP (anhydrous) Deionised Water H₂O < 30 ppm (NMP)
Conductive Additive Carbon Black Carbon Black Per BOM
(C65) (C65)
Mixing Vessel Double planetary Double planetary Vessel clean, dry, certified
mixer mixer

results.question: What is a waste battery according to the Battery Waste Management SOP?
results.original_answer: A waste battery encompasses various types of batteries, including used and/or end-of-life batteries, pre-consumer off-spec batteries, batteries with expired usage dates, and batteries discarded by users.
results.llm_answer: A waste battery is any used, end-of-life, pre-consumer off-spec, discarded, expired, or damaged battery. This includes both primary disposable and secondary rechargeable batteries.
results.context:
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)
Battery Waste Management
1.Purpose: This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is meticulously designed to ensure effective battery
waste management in strict compliance with the Battery Waste Management Rules of 2022. The SOP
applies comprehensively to all units of THDCIL and encompasses the responsibilities and procedures
concerning the collection, segregation, transportation, and disposal of waste batteries. It also outlines the
prohibitions on heavy metal content in batteries.
2. Scope: This SOP is applicable comprehensively to all Projects/Units/Offices of THDCIL.
3. Definitions:
• Battery: Refers to new or refurbished cells and/or batteries and/or their components, including
accumulators, that generate electrical energy through the direct conversion of chemical energy. This
includes disposable primary and/or secondary batteries.
• Waste Battery: Encompasses various types of batteries, including used and/or end-of-life batteries,
pre-consumer off-spec batteries, batteries with expired usage dates, and batteries discarded by users.
4. Responsibility of Waste Generator (i.e. THDCIL):
It is the duty of consumer units/offices within THDCIL to:
• Discard waste batteries separately from other waste streams, especially mixed waste and domestic
waste.
• Ensure that waste batteries are disposed of in an environmentally friendly manner by giving them
to entities engaged in the collection, refurbishment, or recycling of batteries.
• Storage: THDCIL Projects/Units/Offices must store waste batteries in designated containers or
storage areas to prevent leakage and contamination. Separate storage facilities must be provided for
different types of batteries (e.g., primary and secondary batteries) and must comply with safety
regulations.
• Segregation: Waste batteries should be segregated based on their type, chemistry, and heavy metal
content. Separate containers labelled for different battery types (e.g., mercury-containing batteries)
should be used for proper identification.
• Transportation: Transportation of waste batteries should be done using specially designed and
covered transport systems to prevent foul odours, spillage, and environmental contamination.
• Disposal: Waste batteries should be disposed of through authorized entities engaged in battery
collection, refurbishment, or recycling. Ensure compliance with environmental regulations and
guidelines during disposal.
5. Training and Awareness: THDCIL units shall conduct regular training and awareness programs for
employees involved in battery waste management. This training should cover proper storage, segregation,
transportation, disposal procedures, and prohibitions on heavy metal content in batteries.
6. Compliance and Monitoring: THDCIL will diligently monitor compliance with this SOP and conduct
periodic audits to ensure that all Projects/Units/Offices adhere to the Battery Waste Management Rules of
2022. Non-compliance may lead to appropriate disciplinary actions or penalties.
7. Review and Revision: This SoP shall be reviewed annually or as required to ensure its effectiveness
and alignment with environmental regulations.
8. Continuous Improvement:
THDCIL shall continually assess and improve its Battery Waste Management practices to minimize
environmental impact and promote sustainability.

Furthermore, OSHA regulations address hazardous waste operations, which include the
proper management and disposal of lithium batteries. Hazardous Waste Operations and
Emergency Response (HAZWOPER) standards (29 CFR 1910.120(q)) outline procedures for
handling hazardous materials, including lithium batteries, during cleanup operations,
emergency response, and other related activities. Compliance with these standards helps
prevent environmental contamination and ensures the safe handling of hazardous waste.
Fire protection is another crucial aspect addressed by OSHA regulations. Companies
working with lithium batteries must have appropriate fire suppression equipment, fire
detection systems, and alarm systems in place to mitigate the risk of fire incidents. These
measures are essential for early detection and response to fires, protecting employees and
property from harm.
It is important to note that the specific OSHA requirements and regulations applicable to
lithium batteries may vary depending on the business sector and the nature of operations.
Each company is responsible for identifying and implementing the OSHA requirements that
are relevant to their specific practices and ensuring compliance to maintain a safe
workplace.
EPA
Handlers of electric vehicle (EV) and large format batteries are subject to regulations
regarding the generation and management of hazardous waste. The EPA (Environmental
Protection Agency) provides recommendations and guidelines for the proper handling and
disposal of lithium-ion (li-ion) batteries, especially at the end of their life cycle.
The EPA recommends that all li-ion batteries be managed in accordance with the federal
"universal waste" regulations outlined in 40 CFR 273. These regulations provide a framework
for the management, labeling, and storage of universal waste, including li-ion batteries. The
specific requirements within universal waste regulations may vary based on the quantity of
waste generated by a user.
Under universal waste regulations, handlers of li-ion batteries are not required to use
hazardous waste manifests. However, it is mandatory that universal waste is sent to a
permitted hazardous waste facility or to a hazardous waste recycler or universal waste final
destination facility for appropriate disposal or recycling.
Business Requirement – Insurance Providers
The company’s insurance provider is a partner to the company handling EV batteries and
will have a point of view on handling EV batteries at the site from a risk insurance
perspective. This view may align with the AHJ or may differ depending on the level of risk the
insurance provider and company owner are willing to take. It is recommended that
organizations notify their insurance provider regarding the storing or handling EV batteries
on the company premises.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.question: What is a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)?
results.original_answer: Battery electric vehicles (BEV) are powered solely by an electric motor with a battery pack that is recharged from an external source as well as from regenerative braking.
results.llm_answer: A Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) is a type of electric vehicle powered solely by an electric motor. The battery pack, which is recharged from an external power source and through regenerative braking, supplies all the electricity needed to run the vehicle. BEVs do not have an internal combustion engine. When handling a BEV battery, follow all safe handling and storage guidelines to prevent risks.
results.context:
BATTERY IDENTIFICATION
Hybrid vs. Plug-in Hybrid vs. Full Battery Electric vs. Fuel Cell
Hybrid electric vehicles (HEV) are vehicles with an internal combustion engine, an
electric motor, and battery pack. The battery pack is recharged through regenerative
braking.
Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV) are vehicles with an internal combustion engine
and an electric motor along with a battery pack that can be recharged from an
external power source as well as from regenerative braking.
Battery electric vehicles (BEV) are powered solely by an electric motor with a battery
pack that is recharged from an external source as well as from regenerative braking.
Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEV) are also powered solely by an electric motor like a
BEV, but instead of recharging the battery from an external source, they generate their
own electricity when the fuel cell combines hydrogen stored in an onboard tank with
oxygen.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
INTRODUCTION
Companies who handle electric vehicles (EVs) and/or EV batteries after they have been
removed from a vehicle may not have the information or training needed to handle them
safely. Individuals may at times find themselves taking risks in their earnest attempts to do
the right thing. The purpose of this guidance document is to bridge this information gap in a
fast-evolving new industry where real-life case studies and best practices are limited.
This document is written with battery holders in mind, including vehicle dealerships, auto
dismantlers and recyclers, independent garages, auto shredders, warehouse operators,
transportation operators, tow truck operators and yard holders, first responders,
aftermarket diagnostic sites, battery repurposers, and battery remanufactures.
Batteries referenced in this document include lithium-ion (li-ion) electric vehicle traction
batteries for battery electric vehicles (BEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), and
hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) of light duty cars and trucks. Some of the guidance may be
applicable to other types of lithium and nickel metal hydride (NiMH) batteries.
This document is provided for educational purposes only. It is the responsibility of every
company handling EV batteries to understand and comply with all applicable laws and
regulations.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
National Fire Protection Association (NFPA)
NFPA - Emergency Response Guides for Alternative Fuel Vehicles. NFPA maintains a
collection of Emergency Response Guides from 35+ alternative fuel vehicle
manufacturers, which include information on disabling high voltage batteries and
components.
NFPA Emergency Field Guide. This guide covers aspects of electric, hybrid, fuel cell,
and gaseous fuel hazard awareness and procedures — including information from
related NFPA codes.
NFPA Alternative Fuel Vehicles Training Program for Emergency Responders Online
Training. This self-paced online training is designed to teach emergency responders
how to safely deal with emergency situations involving alternative fuel passenger
vehicles, trucks, buses, and commercial fleet vehicles.
Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International
SAE J3235: Best Practices for Storage of Lithium-ion Batteries. SAE J3235 is a
Standard that aids in mitigating risks while storing lithium-ion cells, batteries and
battery systems used in electric vehicles as well as energy storage systems and
other large format industrial applications.
SAE J2950: Recommended Practices for Shipping Transport and Handling of
Automotive-Type Battery System-Lithium Ion. SAE J2950 is a Recommended Practice
(RP) intended to aid in the identification, handling and shipping of lithium-ion
batteries, and also includes recommendations for determining transportability of
possibly damaged/defective batteries.
SAE J2984: Chemical Identification of Transportation Batteries for Recycling. SAE
J2984 is a document that contains a chemistry identification system that can be
used to support recycling rechargeable batteries and battery systems equal to or
greater than 12 volts used in transportation applications.
SAE J2990: Hybrid and EV First and Second Responder Recommended Practice.
This document aims to describe the potential consequences associated with
hazards from xEVs and suggest common procedures to help protect emergency
responders, tow and/or recovery, storage, repair, and salvage personnel after an
incident has occurred with an electrified vehicle.
OEM Manuals / Guides
Information related to an EV battery, including proper handling practices for
emergency responders and dismantlers, is generally provided by OEMs for their
electric vehicles. The links below will direct you to some of the online resources
provided directly by SP OEM members relating to their products.
FCA US LLC (Chrysler, Dodge, Fiat, Jeep, Ram)
Ford / Lincoln
General Motors (Chevrolet, GMC, Buick, Cadillac, Saturn)
Honda / Acura
Toyota / Lexus

Electrode Slurry Mixing — Cathode & Anode
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1
Process Area Electrode Mixing Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 3
1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
4. Materials & Equipment

Active Material NMC811 powder Artificial Graphite Per BOM – batch card
Binder PVDF (Kynar 761) CMC + SBR Battery grade
Solvent NMP (anhydrous) Deionised Water H₂O < 30 ppm (NMP)
Conductive Additive Carbon Black Carbon Black Per BOM
(C65) (C65)
Mixing Vessel Double planetary Double planetary Vessel clean, dry, certified
mixer mixer

Electrode Coating — Slot-Die Coating Process
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 Version v3.0
Process Area Electrode Coating Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 2
1. Purpose
Defines the operating procedure for slot-die coating of electrode slurry onto aluminium (cathode) and
copper (anode) current collector foil. Covers machine start-up, process parameter setting, in-process
monitoring, and shutdown.
2. Safety Requirements
NMP solvent vapour is present around the coating machine. Drying oven operates up
to 150 °C — risk of burns and solvent inhalation. PPE mandatory: heat-resistant
🦺 SAFETY
gloves near oven exits, chemical-resistant gloves near slurry, safety goggles. Confirm
oven door interlocks functional before start-up.
3. Pre-Coating Checks
28. Verify slurry batch ID and viscosity match coating work order. Slurry age must be < 4 hours. If >
4 hours, re-measure viscosity before proceeding.
29. Check foil reel: correct type (Al 16 µm ±1 µm for cathode; Cu 8 µm ±0.5 µm for anode), no
visible defects (wrinkles, holes, scratches).
30. Inspect slot die: clean lips, no dried slurry. Die gap set per recipe. Die bolts torqued to 8 N·m.
31. Prime slurry pump: run until no air bubbles in return line (minimum 2 minutes).
32. Set oven temperatures per recipe. Allow oven to reach set point and stabilise for minimum 15
minutes.
33. Run 0.5 m trial coat. Check coating weight and appearance before production run.
4. HMI Process Parameters — Coating
Parameter Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Alarm / Stop Limit
Coating Speed (m/min) 20–30 25–35 < 18 or > 38: stop line
Die Gap (µm) 150–200 180–220 ±15 µm from target: adjust
Wet Coating Weight (g/m²) 280–320 200–240 ±10%: stop, recheck die
Oven Zone 1 Temp (°C) 80 60 ±5 °C: log; ±10 °C: stop
Oven Zone 2 Temp (°C) 120 90 ±5 °C: log; ±10 °C: stop
Oven Zone 3 Temp (°C) 140 110 ±5 °C: log; ±10 °C: stop

Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.

SBR — shear degrades binder.

22. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
7. Slurry Quality Check

and Supervisor.
Visual Appearance Uniform, no Uniform, no Mix 30 min more and re-inspect.
lumps lumps Lumps persist → reject.
Any batch failing magnetic particle content spec must be rejected immediately. Do not
🛑 CRITICAL transfer to coating. Notify QA Lead and Shift Supervisor within 15 minutes. Retain the
batch for investigation.
8. Slurry Transfer to Coating
23. Filter slurry through 100-mesh filter during transfer to coating vessel.
24. Record transfer volume and slurry batch ID on coating handover form.
25. Seal and label transfer vessel: Batch ID, slurry type, viscosity result, timestamp, operator ID.
26. Deliver to Coating Zone within 4 hours of batch completion. Record transfer timestamp.
27. Clean mixing vessel per Cleaning SOP-ELEC-CLEAN-003 immediately after transfer.
9. Alarms & Responses — Mixing Zone
Alarm Code Description Immediate Action
MIX-ALM-001 Mixer motor Stop mixer. Allow 15 min cool-down. Check for
overtemperature slurry hardening. If motor > 90 °C, call
Maintenance.
MIX-ALM-002 LEV airflow low STOP all solvent handling. Evacuate mixing area.
Do not re-enter until LEV restored. Notify
Maintenance and Supervisor.
MIX-ALM-003 Vessel vacuum loss Pause degassing. Check pump and seals. If
vacuum cannot be restored, degas with extended
low-speed mix (60 min). Record deviation.

Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
General Safety
Q: What is the emergency stop (E-stop) procedure?
A: Press the nearest red mushroom-head E-stop button. This immediately de-energises the machine.
Shout 'E-STOP ACTIVATED' to alert nearby workers. Do NOT restart the machine after an E-stop
without first investigating and resolving the cause, and obtaining Shift Supervisor authorisation. Notify
the Shift Supervisor of every E-stop event.
Q: I can smell strong solvent but no alarm has triggered. What should I do?
A: Evacuate the area immediately and activate the nearest Andon. Do not wait for an alarm. Notify the
Shift Supervisor verbally. This may indicate a LEV failure or solvent spill before the sensor has detected
it. Re-entry only with Supervisor and EHS authorisation. Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §3, Step 3
— when in doubt, stop.
Q: If a quality hold is raised, who do I notify and how quickly?
A: Notify your Shift Supervisor and QA Lead within 15 minutes of raising the hold. Use MES hold
notification plus a verbal communication. Do not release a quality hold without QA Lead sign-off.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §4.
— END OF DOCUMENT — KB-ELEC-SOP-CORPUS-v1.0 — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY —
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

5. Pre-Mixing Checks
All pre-mixing checks must be completed and initialled on the Batch Record before
ℹ NOTE
proceeding. Do not begin weighing until all checks pass.
1. Confirm work order number and batch card match. Record Batch ID on the mixing log.
2. Verify mixing vessel is clean and dry. Check vessel certification tag — must be within 7-day
cleaning validity.
3. Confirm LEV is running: green LED on extraction panel is lit. Do NOT proceed if amber or red.
4. Check calibration status of balance (±0.1 g accuracy required). Confirm calibration sticker is
current.
5. Check calibration status of viscometer. Confirm spindle number matches recipe card.
6. Confirm active material moisture content < 0.1% (check drying oven log for last pre-dry run).
7. Confirm NMP moisture content < 30 ppm. Check certificate of analysis on drum.
8. Record ambient temperature and humidity. Temperature must be 20–25 °C; Humidity < 30%
RH. If out of range, notify Shift Supervisor before proceeding.
6. Mixing Procedure
6.1 Cathode Slurry (NMC)
Add materials in the exact sequence below. Out-of-sequence addition causes
⚡ CAUTION
agglomeration and will require the batch to be scrapped.
9. Weigh PVDF binder per batch card. Add to mixing vessel.
10. Add 60% of total NMP solvent. Mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully
dissolved.
11. Add carbon black (C65). Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes.
12. Add 50% of NMC active material. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
13. Add remaining 50% NMC. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
14. Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity. Mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes.
15. Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry. Maintain low-speed mixing during
degassing.
16. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
6.2 Anode Slurry (Graphite)
17. Dissolve CMC in deionised water (50% of total water). Mix at LOW speed (100 RPM) for 60
minutes.
18. Add carbon black. Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 30 minutes.
19. Add graphite active material in two equal portions, 30 minutes apart. Mix at HIGH speed (1200
RPM).

results.question: What are the responsibilities of THDCIL as a waste generator?
results.original_answer: THDCIL must discard waste batteries separately from other waste streams, especially mixed waste and domestic waste. They must ensure that waste batteries are disposed of in an environmentally friendly manner by giving them to entities engaged in the collection, refurbishment, or recycling of batteries. They must store waste batteries in designated containers to prevent leakage and contamination, segregate batteries based on type, chemistry, and heavy metal content, use specially designed transport systems, and dispose through authorized entities while ensuring compliance with environmental regulations.
results.llm_answer:
THDCIL must: 
1. Discard waste batteries separately from other waste
2. Store waste batteries in designated containers following safety rules
3. Segregate waste batteries by type, chemistry and heavy metal content using labelled containers 
4. Transport waste batteries using covered systems to prevent leaks 
5. Dispose of waste batteries only through authorized collectors or recyclers
6. Follow all environmental regulations and guidelines during disposal
results.context:
Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)
Battery Waste Management
1.Purpose: This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) is meticulously designed to ensure effective battery
waste management in strict compliance with the Battery Waste Management Rules of 2022. The SOP
applies comprehensively to all units of THDCIL and encompasses the responsibilities and procedures
concerning the collection, segregation, transportation, and disposal of waste batteries. It also outlines the
prohibitions on heavy metal content in batteries.
2. Scope: This SOP is applicable comprehensively to all Projects/Units/Offices of THDCIL.
3. Definitions:
• Battery: Refers to new or refurbished cells and/or batteries and/or their components, including
accumulators, that generate electrical energy through the direct conversion of chemical energy. This
includes disposable primary and/or secondary batteries.
• Waste Battery: Encompasses various types of batteries, including used and/or end-of-life batteries,
pre-consumer off-spec batteries, batteries with expired usage dates, and batteries discarded by users.
4. Responsibility of Waste Generator (i.e. THDCIL):
It is the duty of consumer units/offices within THDCIL to:
• Discard waste batteries separately from other waste streams, especially mixed waste and domestic
waste.
• Ensure that waste batteries are disposed of in an environmentally friendly manner by giving them
to entities engaged in the collection, refurbishment, or recycling of batteries.
• Storage: THDCIL Projects/Units/Offices must store waste batteries in designated containers or
storage areas to prevent leakage and contamination. Separate storage facilities must be provided for
different types of batteries (e.g., primary and secondary batteries) and must comply with safety
regulations.
• Segregation: Waste batteries should be segregated based on their type, chemistry, and heavy metal
content. Separate containers labelled for different battery types (e.g., mercury-containing batteries)
should be used for proper identification.
• Transportation: Transportation of waste batteries should be done using specially designed and
covered transport systems to prevent foul odours, spillage, and environmental contamination.
• Disposal: Waste batteries should be disposed of through authorized entities engaged in battery
collection, refurbishment, or recycling. Ensure compliance with environmental regulations and
guidelines during disposal.
5. Training and Awareness: THDCIL units shall conduct regular training and awareness programs for
employees involved in battery waste management. This training should cover proper storage, segregation,
transportation, disposal procedures, and prohibitions on heavy metal content in batteries.
6. Compliance and Monitoring: THDCIL will diligently monitor compliance with this SOP and conduct
periodic audits to ensure that all Projects/Units/Offices adhere to the Battery Waste Management Rules of
2022. Non-compliance may lead to appropriate disciplinary actions or penalties.
7. Review and Revision: This SoP shall be reviewed annually or as required to ensure its effectiveness
and alignment with environmental regulations.
8. Continuous Improvement:
THDCIL shall continually assess and improve its Battery Waste Management practices to minimize
environmental impact and promote sustainability.

Furthermore, OSHA regulations address hazardous waste operations, which include the
proper management and disposal of lithium batteries. Hazardous Waste Operations and
Emergency Response (HAZWOPER) standards (29 CFR 1910.120(q)) outline procedures for
handling hazardous materials, including lithium batteries, during cleanup operations,
emergency response, and other related activities. Compliance with these standards helps
prevent environmental contamination and ensures the safe handling of hazardous waste.
Fire protection is another crucial aspect addressed by OSHA regulations. Companies
working with lithium batteries must have appropriate fire suppression equipment, fire
detection systems, and alarm systems in place to mitigate the risk of fire incidents. These
measures are essential for early detection and response to fires, protecting employees and
property from harm.
It is important to note that the specific OSHA requirements and regulations applicable to
lithium batteries may vary depending on the business sector and the nature of operations.
Each company is responsible for identifying and implementing the OSHA requirements that
are relevant to their specific practices and ensuring compliance to maintain a safe
workplace.
EPA
Handlers of electric vehicle (EV) and large format batteries are subject to regulations
regarding the generation and management of hazardous waste. The EPA (Environmental
Protection Agency) provides recommendations and guidelines for the proper handling and
disposal of lithium-ion (li-ion) batteries, especially at the end of their life cycle.
The EPA recommends that all li-ion batteries be managed in accordance with the federal
"universal waste" regulations outlined in 40 CFR 273. These regulations provide a framework
for the management, labeling, and storage of universal waste, including li-ion batteries. The
specific requirements within universal waste regulations may vary based on the quantity of
waste generated by a user.
Under universal waste regulations, handlers of li-ion batteries are not required to use
hazardous waste manifests. However, it is mandatory that universal waste is sent to a
permitted hazardous waste facility or to a hazardous waste recycler or universal waste final
destination facility for appropriate disposal or recycling.
Business Requirement – Insurance Providers
The company’s insurance provider is a partner to the company handling EV batteries and
will have a point of view on handling EV batteries at the site from a risk insurance
perspective. This view may align with the AHJ or may differ depending on the level of risk the
insurance provider and company owner are willing to take. It is recommended that
organizations notify their insurance provider regarding the storing or handling EV batteries
on the company premises.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.question: What are the safety requirements for the mixing zone?
results.original_answer: Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers. Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone. Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting, wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional, solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use, and no open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
results.llm_answer:
1. Ensure Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) is active before opening any solvent containers. Confirm LEV is working by checking the green indicator light on the control panel.
2. Wear required PPE at all times: 
   - Chemical-resistant nitrile gloves at least 0.15mm thick
   - Safety goggles
   - Chemical-resistant lab coat
3. NMP solvent is a reproductive hazard - PREGNANT WORKERS MUST NOT ENTER THE MIXING ZONE under any circumstances. 
4. Keep all solvent containers tightly sealed when not in active use. 
5. No open flames, sparks, or ignition sources may be present within 5 meters of the mixing area.
6. The emergency eyewash station must be clear and functional at all times.

SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1

1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.

Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

[TABLE]
| SOP ID | KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 | Version | v1.0 |
| -------------- | ------------------- | ----------- | ------------------------ |
| Process Area | All Electrode Zones | Access Tier | Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3 |
| Effective Date | 11/03/2026 | Revision | Rev 1 |
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: How long is the vessel certification tag valid for?
results.original_answer: 7 days
results.llm_answer: The vessel certification tag is valid for 7 days. During this time, the vessel may be used for mixing. If the tag is missing, expired, or unclear, the vessel must be routed for cleaning before use.

Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.

5. Pre-Mixing Checks
All pre-mixing checks must be completed and initialled on the Batch Record before
ℹ NOTE
proceeding. Do not begin weighing until all checks pass.
1. Confirm work order number and batch card match. Record Batch ID on the mixing log.
2. Verify mixing vessel is clean and dry. Check vessel certification tag — must be within 7-day
cleaning validity.
3. Confirm LEV is running: green LED on extraction panel is lit. Do NOT proceed if amber or red.
4. Check calibration status of balance (±0.1 g accuracy required). Confirm calibration sticker is
current.
5. Check calibration status of viscometer. Confirm spindle number matches recipe card.
6. Confirm active material moisture content < 0.1% (check drying oven log for last pre-dry run).
7. Confirm NMP moisture content < 30 ppm. Check certificate of analysis on drum.
8. Record ambient temperature and humidity. Temperature must be 20–25 °C; Humidity < 30%
RH. If out of range, notify Shift Supervisor before proceeding.
6. Mixing Procedure
6.1 Cathode Slurry (NMC)
Add materials in the exact sequence below. Out-of-sequence addition causes
⚡ CAUTION
agglomeration and will require the batch to be scrapped.
9. Weigh PVDF binder per batch card. Add to mixing vessel.
10. Add 60% of total NMP solvent. Mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully
dissolved.
11. Add carbon black (C65). Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes.
12. Add 50% of NMC active material. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
13. Add remaining 50% NMC. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
14. Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity. Mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes.
15. Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry. Maintain low-speed mixing during
degassing.
16. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
6.2 Anode Slurry (Graphite)
17. Dissolve CMC in deionised water (50% of total water). Mix at LOW speed (100 RPM) for 60
minutes.
18. Add carbon black. Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 30 minutes.
19. Add graphite active material in two equal portions, 30 minutes apart. Mix at HIGH speed (1200
RPM).

results.question: What are the key characteristics of a thermal event battery?
results.original_answer: Thermal event batteries exhibit bulging/swelling, emit white/grey smoke, have a distinctive sweet bubble gum-like odor, are hot to touch, show melted plastic, produce audible crackling sounds, may have flames/sparks or visible fire, show presence of burn marks with black or gray fire soot, and display visible charring or scorching on the battery pack.
results.llm_answer:
A thermal event battery exhibits severe damage characteristics. Key signs include:
- Bulging or swelling of the battery pack
- Audible crackling or popping sounds during charging 
- Melted plastic components
- Flames, sparks or visible fire from the battery 
- Battery feeling excessively hot to the touch
- Presence of burn marks, charring, or black/gray fire soot on the battery 
- White/grey smoke emission 
- Odd sweet/bubblegum-like odor 
If any of these signs are detected, do NOT attempt to charge or handle the battery. Immediately contact emergency responders and follow thermal runaway containment and extinguishing procedures.
results.context:
End-of-Life vs. Damaged-Defective vs. Thermal Event Batteries
The US Department of Transportation (DOT) and Transport Canada require the shipper
to accurately indicate whether a battery is classified as end-of-life (EOL) or damaged-
defective (DD). The designation of EOL is assigned to a battery after ensuring the
absence of any damage or defects.
Damaged battery characteristics include:
Dents, punctures, cracks in battery outer shell.
Damage including fluid(s) coming out from within the battery pack.
Water damage and/or corrosion on the terminals*.
Broken or damaged terminals.
Loose wiring either inside the battery pack or protruding from the pack.
Indications the battery has been opened and/or worked on.
Pictured: Damaged power storage unit
battery with examples of loose wiring, soot
from possible heat on electrical panel, and
hard scratches to outer shell from possible
hit.
Source: The Energy Security Agency
*If the electric vehicle and/or battery pack has been damaged due to flooding (salt water or fresh water) do
not attempt to charge or drive the vehicle/battery. Call first responders as the battery pack may now be at risk
of a thermal event.
Thermal event battery characteristics
Thermal event batteries are classified as damaged and exhibit distinct characteristics
that necessitate additional caution, such as:
Bulging/Swelling. Audible crackling sounds.
Melted plastic. Flames/sparks or visible fire.
Hot to touch. Presence of burn marks, along with
Emit white/grey smoke. black or gray fire soot.
A distinctive sweet bubble gum-like odor. Visible charring or scorching on the
battery pack.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
APPENDIX: SELF-ASSESSMENT CHECKLIST
This self-assessment checklist is provided for educational purposes only to assist readers in
reviewing the topics covered in this guidance document.
Battery Identification
End-of-life characteristics identified and published for employees to reference
Damaged-defective characteristics identified and published for employees to
reference
Thermal runaway characteristics identified and published for employees to
reference
Safety Prevention & Event Management
Emergency preparedness plan
Meeting with fire marshal or authority having jurisdiction (AHJ)
Meeting with company’s insurance provider
Employee emergency response training
Employee hazmat shipper certification training
Procurement of appropriate:
Personal protective equipment
Thermal runaway response systems and containment materials
Integration of thermal imaging cameras and 4-gas meters into alarm system
Optional infrastructure upgrades:
Outdoor
Indoor
Battery temporary storage plans
Thermal runaway containment and extinguishing plans
Water runoff management plan
Battery packaging materials for shipping
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.question: How frequently should the SOP be reviewed?
results.original_answer: Annually or as required
results.llm_answer: Review the SOPs annually or whenever a revision is issued by the Engineering team.
results.context:
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
General Safety
Q: What is the emergency stop (E-stop) procedure?
A: Press the nearest red mushroom-head E-stop button. This immediately de-energises the machine.
Shout 'E-STOP ACTIVATED' to alert nearby workers. Do NOT restart the machine after an E-stop
without first investigating and resolving the cause, and obtaining Shift Supervisor authorisation. Notify
the Shift Supervisor of every E-stop event.
Q: I can smell strong solvent but no alarm has triggered. What should I do?
A: Evacuate the area immediately and activate the nearest Andon. Do not wait for an alarm. Notify the
Shift Supervisor verbally. This may indicate a LEV failure or solvent spill before the sensor has detected
it. Re-entry only with Supervisor and EHS authorisation. Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §3, Step 3
— when in doubt, stop.
Q: If a quality hold is raised, who do I notify and how quickly?
A: Notify your Shift Supervisor and QA Lead within 15 minutes of raising the hold. Use MES hold
notification plus a verbal communication. Do not release a quality hold without QA Lead sign-off.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §4.
— END OF DOCUMENT — KB-ELEC-SOP-CORPUS-v1.0 — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY —
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: What should be done if the LEV indicator shows amber or red?
results.original_answer: Do NOT proceed. The green LED on extraction panel must be lit before proceeding.
results.llm_answer: If LEV indicator shows amber or red, stop the procedure immediately. Do not start weighing or mixing. Notify your Shift Supervisor and the EHS team immediately. Do not proceed until LEV is confirmed to be in the green state (green LED on extraction panel). Follow normal safety lockout/tagout protocols.

General Alarm Response & Escalation — Electrode Zone
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
1. Purpose
Defines the standard response framework for all process alarms in the electrode manufacturing area.
All operators must follow this procedure when any alarm is triggered. Machine-specific responses are in
the respective machine SOPs.
2. Alarm Priority Classification
Priority Colour / Sound Definition Response Time
Required
P1 — Critical RED / Continuous Immediate safety or quality risk. Acknowledge within 30
horn Production must stop. seconds.
P2 — High AMBER / Pulsed Process deviation requiring Acknowledge < 2 min.
horn action. May impact quality. Resolve < 10 min.
P3 — Medium YELLOW / Audible Advisory. Monitor closely. Acknowledge < 5 min.
beep Production may continue. Log and monitor.
P4 — Low BLUE / Visual only Informational. No immediate Acknowledge < 15 min.
action required. Log.
3. General Alarm Response Procedure
79. ACKNOWLEDGE the alarm on the HMI within the required response time for its priority class.
80. IDENTIFY the alarm: read alarm code, description, and affected zone on HMI alarm screen.
81. ASSESS: Is production safe to continue? If any doubt, STOP the line. When in doubt, stop.
82. CONSULT the machine-specific SOP for the alarm code (MIX-001, COAT-002, DRY-003, CAL-
004, or SLIT-005).
83. TAKE the action specified in the machine SOP. Do not deviate from the specified response.
84. RECORD the alarm on the Alarm Log: alarm code, time, action taken, resolved time, operator
ID.
85. ESCALATE if alarm cannot be resolved within specified response time — see Section 4.
4. Escalation Matrix
Condition Escalate To Time Limit Method
P1 alarm — any duration Shift Supervisor < 1 minute Verbal + Andon pull

Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification
Viscometer Brookfield DV2T Brookfield DV2T Calibrated, within cert date
5. Pre-Mixing Checks
All pre-mixing checks must be completed and initialled on the Batch Record before
ℹ NOTE
proceeding. Do not begin weighing until all checks pass.
1. Confirm work order number and batch card match. Record Batch ID on the mixing log.
2. Verify mixing vessel is clean and dry. Check vessel certification tag — must be within 7-day
cleaning validity.
3. Confirm LEV is running: green LED on extraction panel is lit. Do NOT proceed if amber or red.
4. Check calibration status of balance (±0.1 g accuracy required). Confirm calibration sticker is
current.
5. Check calibration status of viscometer. Confirm spindle number matches recipe card.
6. Confirm active material moisture content < 0.1% (check drying oven log for last pre-dry run).
7. Confirm NMP moisture content < 30 ppm. Check certificate of analysis on drum.
8. Record ambient temperature and humidity. Temperature must be 20–25 °C; Humidity < 30%
RH. If out of range, notify Shift Supervisor before proceeding.
6. Mixing Procedure
6.1 Cathode Slurry (NMC)
Add materials in the exact sequence below. Out-of-sequence addition causes
⚡ CAUTION
agglomeration and will require the batch to be scrapped.
9. Weigh PVDF binder per batch card. Add to mixing vessel.
10. Add 60% of total NMP solvent. Mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully
dissolved.
11. Add carbon black (C65). Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes.
12. Add 50% of NMC active material. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
13. Add remaining 50% NMC. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
14. Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity. Mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes.
15. Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry. Maintain low-speed mixing during
degassing.
16. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
6.2 Anode Slurry (Graphite)
17. Dissolve CMC in deionised water (50% of total water). Mix at LOW speed (100 RPM) for 60
minutes.
18. Add carbon black. Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 30 minutes.
19. Add graphite active material in two equal portions, 30 minutes apart. Mix at HIGH speed (1200
RPM).

results.question: What is the cathode target viscosity range?
results.llm_answer: The cathode electrode slurry viscosity must be between 4000 and 6000 mPa·s. If readings are outside this range, add solvent in small increments, mix at low speed for 15 minutes, then recheck the viscosity. Make a maximum of two adjustment attempts. If viscosity remains out of specification after two attempts, reject the batch and inform your Shift Supervisor immediately.

SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.

results.question: What binder is used for cathode (NMC) electrodes?
results.original_answer: PVDF (Kynar 761)
results.llm_answer: PVDF (Kynar 761)
results.context:

Electrode Slurry Mixing — Cathode & Anode
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1
Process Area Electrode Mixing Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 3
1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
4. Materials & Equipment
Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification
Active Material NMC811 powder Artificial Graphite Per BOM – batch card
Binder PVDF (Kynar 761) CMC + SBR Battery grade
Solvent NMP (anhydrous) Deionised Water H₂O < 30 ppm (NMP)
Conductive Additive Carbon Black Carbon Black Per BOM
(C65) (C65)
Mixing Vessel Double planetary Double planetary Vessel clean, dry, certified
mixer mixer

5. Pre-Mixing Checks
All pre-mixing checks must be completed and initialled on the Batch Record before
ℹ NOTE
proceeding. Do not begin weighing until all checks pass.
1. Confirm work order number and batch card match. Record Batch ID on the mixing log.
2. Verify mixing vessel is clean and dry. Check vessel certification tag — must be within 7-day
cleaning validity.
3. Confirm LEV is running: green LED on extraction panel is lit. Do NOT proceed if amber or red.
4. Check calibration status of balance (±0.1 g accuracy required). Confirm calibration sticker is
current.
5. Check calibration status of viscometer. Confirm spindle number matches recipe card.
6. Confirm active material moisture content < 0.1% (check drying oven log for last pre-dry run).
7. Confirm NMP moisture content < 30 ppm. Check certificate of analysis on drum.
8. Record ambient temperature and humidity. Temperature must be 20–25 °C; Humidity < 30%
RH. If out of range, notify Shift Supervisor before proceeding.
6. Mixing Procedure
6.1 Cathode Slurry (NMC)
Add materials in the exact sequence below. Out-of-sequence addition causes
⚡ CAUTION
agglomeration and will require the batch to be scrapped.
9. Weigh PVDF binder per batch card. Add to mixing vessel.
10. Add 60% of total NMP solvent. Mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully
dissolved.
11. Add carbon black (C65). Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes.
12. Add 50% of NMC active material. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
13. Add remaining 50% NMC. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
14. Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity. Mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes.
15. Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry. Maintain low-speed mixing during
degassing.
16. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
6.2 Anode Slurry (Graphite)
17. Dissolve CMC in deionised water (50% of total water). Mix at LOW speed (100 RPM) for 60
minutes.
18. Add carbon black. Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 30 minutes.
19. Add graphite active material in two equal portions, 30 minutes apart. Mix at HIGH speed (1200
RPM).
