results.question: What is the SOP ID for Electrode Calendaring?
results.original_answer: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004
results.llm_answer: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004
results.context:
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

Electrode Calendaring — Density & Thickness Control
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 Version v2.2
Process Area Electrode Calendaring Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 2
1. Purpose
Covers the calendaring (compression rolling) process to compact dried electrode coating to target
density and thickness. Calendaring directly affects cell capacity, internal resistance, and electrolyte
wetting.
2. Safety Requirements
Calendaring rolls exert up to 200 kN of force. The roll nip point is a No-Reach zone
during operation. All guarding must be in place and interlocks active. No loose clothing
🦺 SAFETY
or jewellery near rolls. Use push sticks to guide foil — never use hands near nip
points.
3. Pre-Calendaring Checks
45. Verify electrode reel has passed drying quality check. Check reel tag for moisture result and
sign-off. Do NOT calendar undried or OOS moisture reels.
46. Set roll gap per recipe. Verify with feeler gauge on both operator side and drive side. Gap
difference must be < 2 µm.
47. Set roll temperature: Cathode 80 °C, Anode 25 °C (room temperature). Allow heated roll to
stabilise for 20 minutes.
48. Set line speed per recipe. Do not exceed 20 m/min until first 100 m quality check confirmed.
49. Thread leader foil through rolls to verify alignment before loading electrode reel.
4. Process Parameters — Calendaring
Parameter Cathode Target Anode Target OOS Action
Electrode Thickness (µm) 120–130 140–155 Stop, adjust roll gap.
Electrode Density (g/cm³) 3.3–3.5 1.5–1.6 Adjust gap; re-check.
Calendaring Force (kN) 100–180 60–100 Reduce if above maximum.
Roll Temperature (°C) 80 ± 5 25 ± 5 Stop if deviation > ±10 °C.
Line Speed (m/min) 10–20 15–25 Max 20 m/min until QC
confirmed.
Porosity (%) 30–35 38–42 QA hold; notify process
engineer.

Electrode Drying — Inline & Vacuum Oven Process
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 Version v1.4
Process Area Electrode Drying Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
1. Purpose
Defines the controlled drying procedure to remove residual solvent from coated electrodes and achieve
required moisture levels for calendaring and cell assembly. Covers both inline drying and vacuum oven
batch drying.
2. Safety Requirements
Oven surfaces reach up to 150 °C. Thermal burns are a serious risk. Allow 30-minute
🦺 SAFETY cool-down before any maintenance access to oven zones. NMP vapour is extracted
via oven exhaust — verify exhaust fan is running before operating.
3. Inline Drying — Operating Checks
Inline drying occurs automatically within the coating machine oven. Zone temperature targets are set on
the coating HMI — see SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 Table 1.
34. Before starting, verify all three oven zones are at set point. Confirm exhaust flow indicator is
green.
35. Monitor NMP exhaust concentration meter during production. If reading approaches 25% LEL,
stop line and increase ventilation.
36. Check residual solvent in dried electrode every 1000 m using inline IR sensor. Target: < 50 ppm
NMP.
37. If residual NMP > 200 ppm: reduce line speed by 10% and re-check. If still > 200 ppm after
speed reduction, stop line and inspect oven heaters.
4. Vacuum Oven Batch Drying Procedure
Use for: (a) additional drying pass when inline drying fails spec, or (b) electrode reels awaiting coating >
24 hours after slurry application.
38. Pre-heat vacuum oven to 120 °C. Allow temperature to stabilise for 30 minutes.
39. Load electrode reels onto mandrel holders. Do not stack reels — each reel requires its own
holder.
40. Seal oven door. Draw vacuum to < 10 Pa. Record vacuum level at start.
41. Hold at 120 °C / < 10 Pa for minimum 12 hours (cathode) or 8 hours (anode).
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: Compare the electrode thickness targets for cathode and anode.
results.original_answer: Cathode target thickness is 120–130 µm, while anode target thickness is 140–155 µm. Anode electrodes are thicker.
results.llm_answer:
The calibrated electrodes must meet these thickness specifications:
- Cathode: 120 µm to 130 µm 
- Anode: 140 µm to 155 µm

During calendaring, if the measured thickness falls outside these ranges, you must stop production and adjust the roll gap setting until the desired thickness is achieved.

Electrode Drying — Inline & Vacuum Oven Process
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 Version v1.4
Process Area Electrode Drying Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
1. Purpose
Defines the controlled drying procedure to remove residual solvent from coated electrodes and achieve
required moisture levels for calendaring and cell assembly. Covers both inline drying and vacuum oven
batch drying.
2. Safety Requirements
Oven surfaces reach up to 150 °C. Thermal burns are a serious risk. Allow 30-minute
🦺 SAFETY cool-down before any maintenance access to oven zones. NMP vapour is extracted
via oven exhaust — verify exhaust fan is running before operating.
3. Inline Drying — Operating Checks
Inline drying occurs automatically within the coating machine oven. Zone temperature targets are set on
the coating HMI — see SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 Table 1.
34. Before starting, verify all three oven zones are at set point. Confirm exhaust flow indicator is
green.
35. Monitor NMP exhaust concentration meter during production. If reading approaches 25% LEL,
stop line and increase ventilation.
36. Check residual solvent in dried electrode every 1000 m using inline IR sensor. Target: < 50 ppm
NMP.
37. If residual NMP > 200 ppm: reduce line speed by 10% and re-check. If still > 200 ppm after
speed reduction, stop line and inspect oven heaters.
4. Vacuum Oven Batch Drying Procedure
Use for: (a) additional drying pass when inline drying fails spec, or (b) electrode reels awaiting coating >
24 hours after slurry application.
38. Pre-heat vacuum oven to 120 °C. Allow temperature to stabilise for 30 minutes.
39. Load electrode reels onto mandrel holders. Do not stack reels — each reel requires its own
holder.
40. Seal oven door. Draw vacuum to < 10 Pa. Record vacuum level at start.
41. Hold at 120 °C / < 10 Pa for minimum 12 hours (cathode) or 8 hours (anode).

Electrode Slurry Mixing — Cathode & Anode
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1
Process Area Electrode Mixing Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 3
1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
4. Materials & Equipment
Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification

Binder PVDF (Kynar 761) CMC + SBR Battery grade

Conductive Additive Carbon Black Carbon Black Per BOM
(C65) (C65)
Mixing Vessel Double planetary Double planetary Vessel clean, dry, certified
mixer mixer

results.question: What is the difference in electrode density between cathode and anode?
results.original_answer: Cathode density is 3.3–3.5 g/cm³, while anode density is 1.5–1.6 g/cm³. Cathode is more than twice as dense as anode.
results.llm_answer:
The electrode density target ranges are:
- Cathode: 3.3 to 3.5 g/cm³
- Anode: 1.5 to 1.6 g/cm³

Always verify that density readings fall within these specification windows during calendaring and immediately adjust the roll gap if out of spec.

Electrode Calendaring — Density & Thickness Control
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 Version v2.2
Process Area Electrode Calendaring Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 2
1. Purpose
Covers the calendaring (compression rolling) process to compact dried electrode coating to target
density and thickness. Calendaring directly affects cell capacity, internal resistance, and electrolyte
wetting.
2. Safety Requirements
Calendaring rolls exert up to 200 kN of force. The roll nip point is a No-Reach zone
during operation. All guarding must be in place and interlocks active. No loose clothing
🦺 SAFETY
or jewellery near rolls. Use push sticks to guide foil — never use hands near nip
points.
3. Pre-Calendaring Checks
45. Verify electrode reel has passed drying quality check. Check reel tag for moisture result and
sign-off. Do NOT calendar undried or OOS moisture reels.
46. Set roll gap per recipe. Verify with feeler gauge on both operator side and drive side. Gap
difference must be < 2 µm.
47. Set roll temperature: Cathode 80 °C, Anode 25 °C (room temperature). Allow heated roll to
stabilise for 20 minutes.
48. Set line speed per recipe. Do not exceed 20 m/min until first 100 m quality check confirmed.
49. Thread leader foil through rolls to verify alignment before loading electrode reel.
4. Process Parameters — Calendaring
Parameter Cathode Target Anode Target OOS Action
Electrode Thickness (µm) 120–130 140–155 Stop, adjust roll gap.
Electrode Density (g/cm³) 3.3–3.5 1.5–1.6 Adjust gap; re-check.
Calendaring Force (kN) 100–180 60–100 Reduce if above maximum.
Roll Temperature (°C) 80 ± 5 25 ± 5 Stop if deviation > ±10 °C.
Line Speed (m/min) 10–20 15–25 Max 20 m/min until QC
confirmed.
Porosity (%) 30–35 38–42 QA hold; notify process
engineer.

Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: Describe the vacuum oven batch drying procedure.
results.original_answer: Pre-heat vacuum oven to 120°C and allow temperature to stabilize for 30 minutes. Load electrode reels onto mandrel holders without stacking (each reel requires its own holder). Seal oven door and draw vacuum to < 10 Pa, recording vacuum level at start. Hold at 120°C / < 10 Pa for minimum 12 hours (cathode) or 8 hours (anode). After drying cycle, allow oven to cool to < 40°C under vacuum before breaking vacuum. Do NOT break vacuum while oven temperature > 60°C. Transfer dried reels immediately to dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage with maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air.
results.llm_answer:
1. Preheat the vacuum oven to 120 °C and allow it to stabilize for 30 minutes.
2. Load one electrode reel per mandrel holder—do not stack reels.
3. Close the oven door and draw the vacuum down below 10 Pa. Record the vacuum level.
4. Maintain 120 °C and vacuum for a minimum of 12 hours for cathodes or 8 hours for anodes.
5. After drying, let the oven cool below 40 °C while under vacuum before breaking the vacuum seal.
6. Transfer the dried reels immediately to a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Limit ambient exposure to 30 minutes.

results.question: What is an end-of-life battery?
results.original_answer: A used EV battery that has come to the end of its service life in its current vehicle. This battery does not exhibit any signs of damage, defects, or past or present thermal events.
results.llm_answer: An end-of-life battery is a used EV battery that has reached the end of its service life in the current vehicle. It does not show any damage, defects, or signs of past or present thermal events.

General Safety
Q: What is the emergency stop (E-stop) procedure?
A: Press the nearest red mushroom-head E-stop button. This immediately de-energises the machine.
Shout 'E-STOP ACTIVATED' to alert nearby workers. Do NOT restart the machine after an E-stop
without first investigating and resolving the cause, and obtaining Shift Supervisor authorisation. Notify
the Shift Supervisor of every E-stop event.
Q: I can smell strong solvent but no alarm has triggered. What should I do?
A: Evacuate the area immediately and activate the nearest Andon. Do not wait for an alarm. Notify the
Shift Supervisor verbally. This may indicate a LEV failure or solvent spill before the sensor has detected
it. Re-entry only with Supervisor and EHS authorisation. Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §3, Step 3
— when in doubt, stop.
Q: If a quality hold is raised, who do I notify and how quickly?
A: Notify your Shift Supervisor and QA Lead within 15 minutes of raising the hold. Use MES hold
notification plus a verbal communication. Do not release a quality hold without QA Lead sign-off.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §4.

results.question: What is a Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)?
results.original_answer: Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV) are vehicles with an internal combustion engine and an electric motor along with a battery pack that can be recharged from an external power source as well as from regenerative braking.
results.llm_answer: A Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV) is a vehicle with both an internal combustion engine and an electric motor. It has a battery pack that can be recharged from an external power source as well as through regenerative braking while driving. This allows the vehicle to be driven using electric power for shorter trips and the gasoline engine for longer trips.
results.context:
BATTERY IDENTIFICATION
Hybrid vs. Plug-in Hybrid vs. Full Battery Electric vs. Fuel Cell
Hybrid electric vehicles (HEV) are vehicles with an internal combustion engine, an
electric motor, and battery pack. The battery pack is recharged through regenerative
braking.
Plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEV) are vehicles with an internal combustion engine
and an electric motor along with a battery pack that can be recharged from an
external power source as well as from regenerative braking.
Battery electric vehicles (BEV) are powered solely by an electric motor with a battery
pack that is recharged from an external source as well as from regenerative braking.
Fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEV) are also powered solely by an electric motor like a
BEV, but instead of recharging the battery from an external source, they generate their
own electricity when the fuel cell combines hydrogen stored in an onboard tank with
oxygen.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
INTRODUCTION
Companies who handle electric vehicles (EVs) and/or EV batteries after they have been
removed from a vehicle may not have the information or training needed to handle them
safely. Individuals may at times find themselves taking risks in their earnest attempts to do
the right thing. The purpose of this guidance document is to bridge this information gap in a
fast-evolving new industry where real-life case studies and best practices are limited.
This document is written with battery holders in mind, including vehicle dealerships, auto
dismantlers and recyclers, independent garages, auto shredders, warehouse operators,
transportation operators, tow truck operators and yard holders, first responders,
aftermarket diagnostic sites, battery repurposers, and battery remanufactures.
Batteries referenced in this document include lithium-ion (li-ion) electric vehicle traction
batteries for battery electric vehicles (BEVs), plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs), and
hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs) of light duty cars and trucks. Some of the guidance may be
applicable to other types of lithium and nickel metal hydride (NiMH) batteries.
This document is provided for educational purposes only. It is the responsibility of every
company handling EV batteries to understand and comply with all applicable laws and
regulations.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.question: What are all the alarm codes mentioned in the mixing section?
results.original_answer: MIX-ALM-001 (Mixer motor overtemperature), MIX-ALM-002 (LEV airflow low), MIX-ALM-003 (Vessel vacuum loss), and MIX-ALM-004 (Balance communication error)
results.llm_answer: No alarm codes are mentioned in any of the mixing sections. Each section only refers to general safety conditions (LEV light, calibration issues, environmental limits) but does not list specific alarm codes. If you encounter any abnormal conditions during mixing, stop the process and immediately notify your Shift Supervisor.

Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification
Viscometer Brookfield DV2T Brookfield DV2T Calibrated, within cert date
5. Pre-Mixing Checks
All pre-mixing checks must be completed and initialled on the Batch Record before
ℹ NOTE
proceeding. Do not begin weighing until all checks pass.
1. Confirm work order number and batch card match. Record Batch ID on the mixing log.
2. Verify mixing vessel is clean and dry. Check vessel certification tag — must be within 7-day
cleaning validity.
3. Confirm LEV is running: green LED on extraction panel is lit. Do NOT proceed if amber or red.
4. Check calibration status of balance (±0.1 g accuracy required). Confirm calibration sticker is
current.
5. Check calibration status of viscometer. Confirm spindle number matches recipe card.
6. Confirm active material moisture content < 0.1% (check drying oven log for last pre-dry run).
7. Confirm NMP moisture content < 30 ppm. Check certificate of analysis on drum.
8. Record ambient temperature and humidity. Temperature must be 20–25 °C; Humidity < 30%
RH. If out of range, notify Shift Supervisor before proceeding.
6. Mixing Procedure
6.1 Cathode Slurry (NMC)
Add materials in the exact sequence below. Out-of-sequence addition causes
⚡ CAUTION
agglomeration and will require the batch to be scrapped.
9. Weigh PVDF binder per batch card. Add to mixing vessel.
10. Add 60% of total NMP solvent. Mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully
dissolved.
11. Add carbon black (C65). Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes.
12. Add 50% of NMC active material. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
13. Add remaining 50% NMC. Mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes.
14. Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity. Mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes.
15. Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry. Maintain low-speed mixing during
degassing.
16. Proceed to Section 7 — Slurry Quality Check.
6.2 Anode Slurry (Graphite)
17. Dissolve CMC in deionised water (50% of total water). Mix at LOW speed (100 RPM) for 60
minutes.
18. Add carbon black. Mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 30 minutes.
19. Add graphite active material in two equal portions, 30 minutes apart. Mix at HIGH speed (1200
RPM).

results.question: What happens if residual NMP exceeds 200 ppm after inline drying?
results.original_answer: Reduce line speed by 10% and re-check. If still > 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop line and inspect oven heaters. Route reel to vacuum oven. If > 500 ppm, notify Supervisor and hold reel — do not pass to calendaring.
results.llm_answer:
If the inline IR sensor reads residual NMP above 200 ppm after the inline drying step, first reduce the line speed by 10%. Recheck the NMP reading. 

If after the speed reduction the NMP is still above 200 ppm, stop the line immediately. Do NOT pass the electrode to calendaring. Inspect the oven heaters for malfunction. Notify your Supervisor of the issue.

NOT break vacuum while oven temperature > 60 °C.

exposure to ambient air: 30 minutes.
44. Measure residual moisture by Karl Fischer titration from each reel. Target: < 50 ppm H₂O
(cathode); < 100 ppm H₂O (anode).

Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.

Electrode Drying — Inline & Vacuum Oven Process
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 Version v1.4
Process Area Electrode Drying Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
1. Purpose
Defines the controlled drying procedure to remove residual solvent from coated electrodes and achieve
required moisture levels for calendaring and cell assembly. Covers both inline drying and vacuum oven
batch drying.
2. Safety Requirements
Oven surfaces reach up to 150 °C. Thermal burns are a serious risk. Allow 30-minute
🦺 SAFETY cool-down before any maintenance access to oven zones. NMP vapour is extracted
via oven exhaust — verify exhaust fan is running before operating.
3. Inline Drying — Operating Checks
Inline drying occurs automatically within the coating machine oven. Zone temperature targets are set on
the coating HMI — see SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 Table 1.
34. Before starting, verify all three oven zones are at set point. Confirm exhaust flow indicator is
green.
35. Monitor NMP exhaust concentration meter during production. If reading approaches 25% LEL,
stop line and increase ventilation.
36. Check residual solvent in dried electrode every 1000 m using inline IR sensor. Target: < 50 ppm
NMP.
37. If residual NMP > 200 ppm: reduce line speed by 10% and re-check. If still > 200 ppm after
speed reduction, stop line and inspect oven heaters.
4. Vacuum Oven Batch Drying Procedure
Use for: (a) additional drying pass when inline drying fails spec, or (b) electrode reels awaiting coating >
24 hours after slurry application.
38. Pre-heat vacuum oven to 120 °C. Allow temperature to stabilise for 30 minutes.
39. Load electrode reels onto mandrel holders. Do not stack reels — each reel requires its own
holder.
40. Seal oven door. Draw vacuum to < 10 Pa. Record vacuum level at start.
41. Hold at 120 °C / < 10 Pa for minimum 12 hours (cathode) or 8 hours (anode).
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: What is the target porosity range for cathode electrodes?
results.llm_answer: For cathode electrodes, the target porosity range during calendaring is 30-35%. If porosity is outside this range, report the deviation to the process engineer.

Electrode Calendaring — Density & Thickness Control
SOP ID SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 Version v2.2
Process Area Electrode Calendaring Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 2
1. Purpose
Covers the calendaring (compression rolling) process to compact dried electrode coating to target
density and thickness. Calendaring directly affects cell capacity, internal resistance, and electrolyte
wetting.
2. Safety Requirements
Calendaring rolls exert up to 200 kN of force. The roll nip point is a No-Reach zone
during operation. All guarding must be in place and interlocks active. No loose clothing
🦺 SAFETY
or jewellery near rolls. Use push sticks to guide foil — never use hands near nip
points.
3. Pre-Calendaring Checks
45. Verify electrode reel has passed drying quality check. Check reel tag for moisture result and
sign-off. Do NOT calendar undried or OOS moisture reels.
46. Set roll gap per recipe. Verify with feeler gauge on both operator side and drive side. Gap
difference must be < 2 µm.
47. Set roll temperature: Cathode 80 °C, Anode 25 °C (room temperature). Allow heated roll to
stabilise for 20 minutes.
48. Set line speed per recipe. Do not exceed 20 m/min until first 100 m quality check confirmed.
49. Thread leader foil through rolls to verify alignment before loading electrode reel.
4. Process Parameters — Calendaring
Parameter Cathode Target Anode Target OOS Action
Electrode Thickness (µm) 120–130 140–155 Stop, adjust roll gap.
Electrode Density (g/cm³) 3.3–3.5 1.5–1.6 Adjust gap; re-check.
Calendaring Force (kN) 100–180 60–100 Reduce if above maximum.
Roll Temperature (°C) 80 ± 5 25 ± 5 Stop if deviation > ±10 °C.
Line Speed (m/min) 10–20 15–25 Max 20 m/min until QC
confirmed.
Porosity (%) 30–35 38–42 QA hold; notify process
engineer.

notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: What is the complete safety protocol for handling NMP in the facility?
results.original_answer: NMP safety spans multiple processes: In mixing (Page 2), ensure LEV is active, wear chemical-resistant gloves and PPE, pregnant workers must not enter, no open flames within 5m. In coating (Page 6), NMP vapor is present around coating machine, use heat-resistant gloves near oven, confirm oven interlocks functional. In drying (Page 8), monitor NMP exhaust concentration (stop if approaching 25% LEL), target residual NMP < 50 ppm in dried electrode, verify exhaust fan running. Throughout all processes, maintain proper ventilation and monitor for vapor concentrations.
results.llm_answer: The complete safety manual (Document 1) does not give a specific written protocol for handling NMP. Refer to Document 3 for NMP requirements. Store spent solvent in sealed containers and label them clearly. Do not pour NMP down drains.
results.context:
SAFETY PREVENTION
Emergency Preparedness Plan
Developing an emergency preparedness plan is essential to mitigate risks associated with
lithium-ion batteries. It is important to customize the plan according to specific facility
requirements and adhere to local regulations. An emergency preparedness plan may
contain several components. Consult with local regulations and authorities, such as local
fire marshals or officials, to tailor the plan to the specific facility and jurisdiction of
operations.
Hazard Assessment:
Batteries may be at varying states of charge (measured in kilowatt-hours), physical size
and shape, which may impact the response, personal protective equipment (PPE)
required, training involved, and tools needed.
Conduct a thorough hazard assessment to identify potential risks associated with
lithium-ion batteries in material handling operations. Document and address these
hazards in the emergency preparedness plan.
Emergency Response Team:
Establish an emergency response team and assign clear responsibilities for
implementing the fire prevention plan and conducting emergency drills.
Designate trained personnel who will handle specific roles during emergencies.
Make sure appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) is available.
Training and Education:
Provide training to all personnel on lithium-ion battery hazards, emergency response
procedures, and proper use of fire suppression equipment.
Conduct regular refresher training sessions to reinforce knowledge and keep personnel
up to date with evolving safety practices.
Response Plan for Thermal Runaway Events and Chemical Spills:
Develop a plan for responding to thermal runaway fires, chemical leaks, and/or off gas
emissions resulting from lithium-ion batteries. Refer to the relevant section of the
emergency preparedness plan for specific guidance on managing such events.
Communication and Alarms:
Establish a communication protocol to ensure timely alerting of personnel during
emergencies.
Install a fire alarm system equipped with smoke detectors, heat detectors (such as
thermal cameras), and gas detectors (such as 4-gas monitors). Position manual call
points at appropriate locations for activation when needed.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 v2.1 | Electrode Slurry MixingCONFIDENTIAL — AGRATAS GIGAFACTORY | INTERNAL USE ONLY
Q: How do I verify the roll gap is equal on both sides?
A: Use a feeler gauge on both the operator side and the drive side of the roll gap. The difference
between the two sides must be less than 2 µm. If the difference is greater, adjust the drive-side screw
until both sides are within 2 µm of each other. Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §3, Step 2.
Q: What do I do if the calendaring force exceeds 200 kN?
A: This is alarm CAL-ALM-002. Stop the line immediately. Reduce the roll gap setting by 5 µm. Re-
thread the foil and restart at low speed. Do not continue calendaring with force above 200 kN.
Reference: SOP-ELEC-CAL-004 §6.
Slitting
Q: When do I need to change the slitting blades?
A: Replace the blade set when cumulative cut length reaches 50,000 m, or immediately if edge burr
height exceeds 10 µm at any point during the shift. Check the blade log for current mileage at the start
of each shift. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §3, Step 1.
Q: What should I do with a slit reel that has failed the width check?
A: Label the reel clearly as 'HOLD — QC REVIEW'. Move it to the designated hold area immediately.
Do not mix it with conforming reels. Record the reel ID, width measurement, and time on the Slitting
Log, then notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 2.
Q: How soon must conforming slit reels be transferred to cell assembly?
A: Within 8 hours of completion, in sealed packaging. This prevents moisture uptake which could
degrade electrode performance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-SLIT-005 §6, Step 3.
Shift Handover
Q: Can I leave the zone before the incoming operator signs the handover record?
A: No. You must remain in the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged the handover
verbally AND signed the MES Shift Handover Record. Leaving without a completed handover is a non-
conformance. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §2.2, Step 4.
Q: What fields must I complete on the Shift Handover Record?
A: All fields are mandatory: Production Order Number, Current Reel/Batch ID, Production Output
(metres), Open Alarms (codes + status), Quality Holds (reel IDs + reason), Equipment Restrictions,
Consumable Status, Pending Actions, and both operator signatures. An incomplete handover record
must be flagged to the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-HANDOVER-006 §4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: What are the key components of an Emergency Preparedness Plan for lithium-ion batteries?
results.original_answer: An Emergency Preparedness Plan should include: Hazard Assessment (conduct thorough assessment and document risks), Emergency Response Team (establish team with clear responsibilities and appropriate PPE), Training and Education (provide training on hazards and emergency procedures with regular refresher sessions), Response Plan for Thermal Runaway Events and Chemical Spills (develop specific plans for fires, leaks, and emissions), and Communication and Alarms (establish protocol for timely alerting of personnel).
results.llm_answer:
An Emergency Preparedness Plan for lithium-ion batteries should include:

1. Hazard assessment to identify risks specific to your facility and the varying states of charge, size and shape of batteries.
2. Designating an emergency response team with clear roles and responsibilities. Provide appropriate PPE.
3. Training all staff on lithium-ion battery hazards, response procedures, and fire suppression equipment. Conduct regular refresher sessions.
4. Developing a plan for thermal runaway fires, chemical leaks and off-gas emissions. Include communication protocols and alarms like smoke, heat and gas detectors. 
5. Emergency evacuation procedures with designated assembly points. Conduct regular drills and share the plan with the local fire department. 
6. Maintaining records of training, drills, incidents and updates. Regularly review and update the plan based on lessons learned and changes.
results.context:
SAFETY PREVENTION
Emergency Preparedness Plan
Developing an emergency preparedness plan is essential to mitigate risks associated with
lithium-ion batteries. It is important to customize the plan according to specific facility
requirements and adhere to local regulations. An emergency preparedness plan may
contain several components. Consult with local regulations and authorities, such as local
fire marshals or officials, to tailor the plan to the specific facility and jurisdiction of
operations.

and shape, which may impact the response, personal protective equipment (PPE)
required, training involved, and tools needed.

lithium-ion batteries in material handling operations. Document and address these
hazards in the emergency preparedness plan.

implementing the fire prevention plan and conducting emergency drills.
Designate trained personnel who will handle specific roles during emergencies.
Make sure appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) is available.
Training and Education:
Provide training to all personnel on lithium-ion battery hazards, emergency response
procedures, and proper use of fire suppression equipment.
Conduct regular refresher training sessions to reinforce knowledge and keep personnel
up to date with evolving safety practices.
Response Plan for Thermal Runaway Events and Chemical Spills:
Develop a plan for responding to thermal runaway fires, chemical leaks, and/or off gas
emissions resulting from lithium-ion batteries. Refer to the relevant section of the
emergency preparedness plan for specific guidance on managing such events.
Communication and Alarms:
Establish a communication protocol to ensure timely alerting of personnel during
emergencies.
Install a fire alarm system equipped with smoke detectors, heat detectors (such as
thermal cameras), and gas detectors (such as 4-gas monitors). Position manual call
points at appropriate locations for activation when needed.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
ROLES OF AUTHORITIES
Fire Marshals
The local fire marshal and/or authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) has the final say on how the
site is prepared for handling EVs and EV batteries. Having an emergency preparedness plan
and the physical site reviewed by the AHJ is a very good idea and in many jurisdictions
required by law. The recommendations of one AHJ may differ from an AHJ in another
jurisdiction.
Department of Transportation (DOT)
The Department of Transportation (DOT) plays a role in governing and regulating the proper
shipping and handling of lithium-ion (li-ion) batteries. Their regulations, specifically the
Hazardous Materials Regulations (HMR) (49 CFR Parts 100-180)(49 CFR 173.185), are
enforced by the Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration (PHMSA) division of
the DOT. These regulations ensure the safe transportation of hazardous materials, including
li-ion batteries, by various modes of transportation such as highway, rail, vessel, and air.
The HMR sets forth comprehensive guidelines and requirements for the packaging, labeling,
marking, and documentation of hazardous materials, including li-ion batteries, to prevent
accidents, leaks, or other incidents during transportation. These regulations cover aspects
such as proper training, packaging design, and handling procedures to minimize the risks
associated with transporting hazardous materials. The DOT regulations governing training
can be found in 49 CFR §172.704. These requirements apply to employees designated as
Hazardous Materials Employees and ensure that they receive adequate training on the safe
handling, packaging, and transportation of hazardous materials, including li-ion batteries.
Training programs focus on educating employees about the potential hazards, emergency
response protocols, and compliance with relevant regulations to ensure the safe
transportation of hazardous materials.
OSHA
Companies engaged in the production, storage, or handling of lithium batteries are required
to comply with OSHA standards to ensure workplace safety. OSHA (Occupational Safety
and Health Administration) is a regulatory agency that establishes and enforces standards
to protect the health and safety of workers. Under OSHA regulations, employees involved in
dealing with lithium-ion batteries must receive training on Hazardous Materials Regulations
(HAZMAT) that are relevant to their job functions. This training ensures that employees are
knowledgeable about the potential hazards associated with lithium batteries and are
equipped to handle them safely. Compliance with these regulations helps mitigate risks and
promotes a safe working environment.
In addition to HAZMAT training, OSHA regulations encompass other important aspects
related to the handling of lithium batteries. Emergency planning requirements are mandated
under 29 CFR 1910.38 and 29 CFR 1926.35. These requirements necessitate the development
and implementation of effective emergency response plans in case of incidents or accidents
involving lithium batteries. Such plans ensure that employees are prepared to respond
appropriately to emergencies, minimizing potential injuries or property damage.
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023
Fire Suppression Systems and Equipment:
Consider appropriate fire suppression systems, such as automatic sprinklers and/or
robotic fire suppression units, based on the unique needs of the facility.
Ensure fire suppression systems are strategically placed near lithium-ion battery
storage, charging, and maintenance areas, and any additional areas posing a higher
risk. Note that fire extinguishers are generally ineffective in extinguishing a lithium-ion
battery pack fire.
Housekeeping and Maintenance:
Maintain a clean and organized facility, eliminating flammable materials and clutter
that may contribute to fire hazards.
Regularly inspect and maintain lithium-ion battery storage areas.
Train personnel on safe battery handling practices and emphasize the importance of
proper housekeeping in mitigating risks.
Evacuation and Emergency Procedures:
Establish clear evacuation procedures, including designated assembly points, and
ensure all personnel are familiar with them.
Conduct regular emergency drills to test the effectiveness of evacuation procedures
and identify areas for improvement.
Share emergency response procedures with the local fire department and provide a
tour of the facility to support development of their response plan.
Keep muster points away from areas where at-risk batteries are stored/sheltered
outside.
Documentation and Review:
Maintain records of training sessions, emergency drills, incident reports, and any
modifications made to the emergency preparedness and fire prevention plan.
Regularly review and update the plan based on lessons learned, industry best
practices, and regulatory changes to ensure its continued effectiveness.
Additional Resources and Standards:
OSHA's guidelines on Emergency Action Plans (29 CFR 1910.38)
NFPA 1: Fire Code
NFPA 10: Standard for Portable Fire Extinguishers
NFPA 72: National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code
NFPA 400: Hazardous Materials Code
NFPA - Emergency Response Guides for Alternative Fuel Vehicles
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.category: SOP - How do I do this
results.question: How do I prepare cathode slurry?
results.original_answer: Step 1: Weigh PVDF binder per batch card and add to mixing vessel. Step 2: Add 60% of total NMP solvent and mix at LOW speed (50 RPM) for 30 minutes until PVDF fully dissolved. Step 3: Add carbon black (C65) and mix at MEDIUM speed (500 RPM) for 45 minutes. Step 4: Add 50% of NMC active material and mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes. Step 5: Add remaining 50% NMC and mix at HIGH speed (1500 RPM) for 60 minutes. Step 6: Add remaining 40% NMP to adjust viscosity and mix at MEDIUM speed (800 RPM) for 20 minutes. Step 7: Apply vacuum (−0.08 MPa) for 30 minutes to degas slurry while maintaining low-speed mixing. Step 8: Proceed to slurry quality check.
results.llm_answer:
To prepare cathode slurry:
1. Gather required materials: Cathode active material, conductive additive (carbon black), binder (PVDF), NMP solvent, and rheology modifier (optional) in correct proportions.
2. Add active material into mixing vessel.
3. Add carbon black and PVDF binder.
4. Tumble dry mix for 15 minutes at low speed.
5. Slowly add NMP solvent while mixing on medium speed until slurry viscosity reaches target range (200-400 mPa.s). Adjust rheology modifier if needed.
6. Use a sieve to remove any lumps if sieve leakage appears.
7. Verify slurry viscosity with viscometer, record value, and confirm within spec before proceeding.

Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

results.question: Compare the safety requirements between electrode manufacturing and EV battery handling.
results.original_answer: Electrode manufacturing (Agratas SOP) focuses on chemical hazards like NMP solvent vapors, requiring LEV systems, chemical-resistant PPE, and specific temperature/humidity controls. EV battery handling (Suppliers Partnership) emphasizes thermal runaway risks, requiring emergency preparedness plans, thermal event monitoring, and proper storage/handling of damaged batteries. Both require comprehensive training and PPE, but manufacturing focuses on process control while battery handling focuses on damage prevention and emergency response.
results.llm_answer:
### Key Differences in Safety Requirements
#### Electrode Manufacturing (NMP solvent):
- **Ventilation**: Must use local exhaust ventilation (LEV) before opening solvent containers. Ensure LEV is running - check green indicator panel.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)**: Mandatory chemical-resistant gloves (minimum 0.15mm thickness), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat. Nitrile gloves specifically required.
- **Flame Restrictions**: No open flames or ignition sources within 5 meters of mixing area.
- **NMP Hazard**: NMP is a reproductive hazard. Pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
- **Container Seals**: Solvent containers must be sealed when not actively in use.

#### EV Battery Handling:
- **Training**: Only "qualified persons" (OSHA definition) should work on batteries over 50V. Requires OSHA HAZMAT training, NFPA 70E compliance, and SAE J2990 standards.
- **PPE**: Mandatory safety glasses, insulated electrical gloves, fire-resistant clothing (Category 2 coveralls, faceshield, hard hat, balaclava). Category 2 coveralls, faceshield, hard hat, balaclava, Class 0 rubber gloves, and leather protectors.
- **Ventilation**: Must use well-ventilated rooms or enclosures with outside vents.
- **Storage**: Batteries must be stored in enclosures with proper ventilation. Use racks/trays resistant to electrolyte.
- **Acid-Resistant Protection**: Floors must be acid-resistant or protected from accumulations. 
- **Emergency Facilities**: Quick drench facilities within 25ft for eye/body flushing, fire protection nearby.
- **Jewelry Removal**: Remove metal objects that could contact electricity.

### Common Safety Requirements
- Both require proper ventilation and PPE use
- Both mandate following to applicable safety standards (OSHA, NFPA 70E)
- Emergency eye wash and body drench facilities required in both settings
- Training and qualified personnel mandatory
- Strict prohibitions on ignition sources and open flames

SOP ID SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 Version v2.1
Process Area Electrode Mixing Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 3
1. Purpose
This SOP defines the controlled procedure for preparing cathode and anode electrode slurries used in
lithium-ion battery manufacturing. Adherence is mandatory to ensure consistent slurry homogeneity,
correct viscosity, and repeatable coating performance.
2. Scope
Applicable to all Line Operators and Shift Supervisors assigned to the Mixing Zone. Covers batch
preparation from dry material weighing through final slurry quality check and transfer to coating.
3. Safety Requirements
Solvent vapours (NMP for cathode, water-based for anode) are present in the mixing
zone. Ensure LEV (Local Exhaust Ventilation) is active before opening any containers.
🦺 SAFETY
Wear chemical-resistant gloves, safety goggles, and lab coat at all times. NMP is a
reproductive hazard — pregnant workers must not enter the mixing zone.
• Verify LEV extraction is operating before starting — check green indicator on panel.
• Wear nitrile gloves (min. 0.15 mm), safety goggles, and chemical-resistant lab coat.
• Keep emergency eyewash station clear and functional.
• Solvent containers must be sealed when not in active use.
• No open flames or ignition sources within 5 m of mixing area.
4. Materials & Equipment
Material / Equipment Cathode (NMC) Anode (Graphite) Specification
Active Material NMC811 powder Artificial Graphite Per BOM – batch card
Binder PVDF (Kynar 761) CMC + SBR Battery grade
Solvent NMP (anhydrous) Deionised Water H₂O < 30 ppm (NMP)
Conductive Additive Carbon Black Carbon Black Per BOM
(C65) (C65)
Mixing Vessel Double planetary Double planetary Vessel clean, dry, certified
mixer mixer

Shift Handover — Electrode Manufacturing
SOP ID SOP-ELEC- Version v1.2
HANDOVER-006
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
1. Purpose
Defines the structured shift handover process for the electrode manufacturing area to ensure
operational continuity, clear communication of status, and accurate transfer of accountability between
outgoing and incoming shifts.
2. Outgoing Shift — Responsibilities
2.1 — 30 Minutes Before Shift End
66. Complete all active in-process quality checks. Record results on run records before handover.
67. Bring current production order to a safe stopping point. Do not initiate a new batch in the last 30
minutes unless instructed by Supervisor.
68. Note all active alarms, deviations, or quality holds: alarm code, time raised, action taken, current
status.
69. Record consumable levels: slurry volume remaining, foil reel length remaining, blade mileage.
2.2 — At Shift Handover
70. Walk the incoming operator through the line. Point out any physical issues (unusual noise,
smell, leaks).
71. Verbally communicate: current production order status, active quality holds, equipment
restrictions.
72. Complete the Shift Handover Record on the MES system. All fields mandatory.
73. Do not leave the zone until the incoming operator has acknowledged handover verbally and
signed the handover record.
3. Incoming Shift — Responsibilities
74. Arrive at zone 10 minutes before shift start. Review the MES Shift Handover Record before
accepting handover.
75. Check all open alarms and quality holds listed in the handover record.
76. Physically inspect the line: equipment status lights, visible leaks, consumable levels.
77. Sign the handover record to accept accountability.
78. If you have concerns about the handover state, notify the Shift Supervisor before accepting
handover.

Safe Handling of EV Batteries
High-voltage batteries can present significant risks if mishandled. Lithium-ion EV batteries
can pose chemical, thermal, and electrical hazards. Handlers of EV batteries can be
divided into two groups: those a) responsible for the movement and storage of the
batteries and b) responsible for the work performed on the battery including its removal
from a vehicle. It is recommended that personnel involved in battery handling take the
following guidelines into consideration.
Training and Qualification:
Following US Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) regulations, only
"qualified persons" should handle batteries with voltages exceeding 50V.
Qualified individuals should have experience and training in working with electricity,
possessing knowledge about electrical hazards and safety practices.
All personnel handling EV batteries must receive appropriate training on safe
operating procedures, hazards, and emergency response protocols.
OSHA HAZMAT training, including general awareness training, function-specific
training, safety training, security awareness training, and in-depth security training, is
required.
See Standards for Electrical Safety in the Workplace: NFPA 70E Article 110.6, OSHA 29
CFR 1910.332 "Training" and Hybrid and EV First and Second Responder Recommended
Practice SAE J2990.
Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):
Wear appropriate PPE during battery handling, including safety glasses, electrical
insulated gloves, and fire-resistant clothing OSHA 1926.441(g)(5) Example: Category 2
coveralls, faceshield, hard hat, and balaclava, plus Class 0 rubber voltage gloves and
leather protector gloves combination.
Suggest removing jewelry and other metal that could come in contact with electricity
and other metal conductive parts of the battery and tools.
Employee Safety:
Facilities handling li-ion batteries must adhere to OSHA requirements.
Those requirements include ensuring that batteries are stored in enclosures with outside
vents or well-ventilated rooms to prevent the escape of fumes, gases, or electrolyte
spray into other areas (1926.441(a)(1)).
Ventilation systems should be in place to facilitate the diffusion of gases and prevent
the accumulation of explosive mixtures (1926.441(a)(2)).
Use substantial racks and trays that are resistant to the electrolyte (1926.441(a)(3)).
Construct floors of acid-resistant materials or protect them from acid accumulations
(1926.441(a)(4)).
Provide facilities within 25 feet (7.62 m) of battery handling areas for quick drenching
of the eyes and body (1926.441(a)(6)).
Ensure the availability of facilities to flush and neutralize spilled electrolyte and for fire
protection (1926.441(a)(7)).
EV BATTERY SAFE HANDLING & STORAGE GUIDANCE | JULY 2023

results.category: SOP - How do I do this
results.question: What are the steps to start coating process?
results.original_answer: Step 1: Verify slurry batch ID and viscosity match coating work order. Slurry age must be < 4 hours. Step 2: Check foil reel for correct type (Al 16 µm ±1 µm for cathode; Cu 8 µm ±0.5 µm for anode) with no visible defects. Step 3: Inspect slot die - clean lips, no dried slurry, die gap set per recipe, die bolts torqued to 8 N·m. Step 4: Prime slurry pump - run until no air bubbles in return line (minimum 2 minutes). Step 5: Set oven temperatures per recipe and allow oven to reach set point and stabilize for minimum 15 minutes. Step 6: Run 0.5 m trial coat and check coating weight and appearance before production run.
results.llm_answer:
Start coating by:

1. Verifying the slurry batch ID and that its viscosity is within spec for the work order. Reject if viscosity is out of spec.
2. Inspecting the foil reel for the correct material (Al for cathode, Cu for anode) and making sure there are no defects like wrinkles or holes.
3. Checking that the slot die is clean, with no dried slurry on the lips, and that the die gap is set to the recipe value with bolts torqued to 8 N·m.
4. Priming the slurry pump for at least 2 minutes, ensuring no air bubbles remain in the return line.
5. Setting oven temperatures according to the recipe, waiting at least 15 minutes for the ovens to reach and stabilise at set point.
6. Running a 0.5 m trial coat to verify the coating weight and appearance before beginning full production coating.

Operator FAQ — Electrode Manufacturing Knowledge Hub
SOP ID KB-ELEC-FAQ-001 Version v1.0
Process Area All Electrode Zones Access Tier Tier 1 / Tier 2 / Tier 3
Effective Date 11/03/2026 Revision Rev 1
This FAQ provides quick answers to common operator questions. All answers link to the authoritative
SOP. When in doubt, always refer to the full SOP.
Mixing
Q: What do I do if the slurry viscosity is too high after mixing?
A: Add small increments of solvent (NMP for cathode; deionised water for anode). Mix at medium
speed for 15 minutes after each addition, then re-measure viscosity. Maximum 2 adjustment attempts.
If still OOS after 2 attempts, reject the batch and notify your Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
MIX-001 §7.
Q: Can I start mixing if ambient humidity is above 30% RH?
A: No. Mixing must not start if humidity exceeds 30% RH. NMP absorbs moisture which degrades slurry
quality. Log the environmental condition, notify your Shift Supervisor, and wait until humidity is within
range. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 8.
Q: The LEV green light is not lit. What do I do?
A: Do NOT open any solvent containers or start mixing. This is a P1 safety condition. Notify your Shift
Supervisor immediately and activate the Andon. Do not re-enter the mixing zone until LEV is confirmed
operational. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §3 and SOP-ELEC-ALARM-007 §2.
Q: How do I know if the mixing vessel is clean enough to use?
A: Check the vessel certification tag on the vessel. The cleaning date must be within the 7-day cleaning
validity window. If the tag is absent, expired, or unclear, treat the vessel as unclean and route for
cleaning before use. Reference: SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 §5, Step 2.
Coating
Q: How often do I check coating weight during a production run?
A: Check wet coating weight every 200 m using the inline beta gauge plus a manual patch sample.
Check dry coating weight every 500 m by cutting a 100 cm² patch, weighing it, and subtracting the foil
weight. Record all results on the Coating Run Record. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §5.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

Q: I can see pinholes in the coating. What should I do?
A: Stop the line if you observe more than 5 pinholes per 100 m². Check the slurry for air bubbles — it
may need to be re-degassed. Inspect and clean the die lip for partial blockage. This corresponds to
alarm code COAT-ALM-005. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §6.
Q: How long can slurry wait before I coat with it?
A: Maximum 4 hours from slurry batch completion to start of coating. If the slurry is older than 4 hours,
re-measure viscosity before proceeding. If viscosity is OOS, do not coat — reject the slurry and notify
the Shift Supervisor. Reference: SOP-ELEC-COAT-002 §3, Step 1.
Q: What do I do if the oven temperature goes outside the alarm limit?
A: A deviation of ±5 °C from set point requires logging and monitoring. A deviation of ±10 °C requires
stopping the coating line. Do not advance foil through the oven. Check the heater and temperature
controller, and notify Maintenance if the fault persists for more than 10 minutes. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-COAT-002 §6, COAT-ALM-002.
Drying
Q: What is the maximum residual NMP allowed in a dried cathode electrode?
A: Less than 50 ppm NMP. If the inline IR sensor reads above 200 ppm, reduce line speed by 10% and
re-check. If residual NMP remains above 200 ppm after speed reduction, stop the line and inspect the
oven heaters. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003 §3.
Q: How long must cathode reels dry in the vacuum oven?
A: Minimum 12 hours at 120 °C with vacuum maintained below 10 Pa. Anode reels require a minimum
of 8 hours. Do not break the vacuum until the oven has cooled below 40 °C. Reference: SOP-ELEC-
DRY-003 §4.
Q: How long can a dried reel be exposed to ambient air before it must be used?
A: Maximum 30 minutes exposure to ambient air after the vacuum oven drying cycle. After that, the reel
must be stored in a dry room (RH < 1%) or nitrogen-purged storage. Reference: SOP-ELEC-DRY-003
§4, Step 6.
Calendaring
Q: The electrode coating is cracking at the rolls. What is the likely cause and what
should I do?
A: Cracking is most commonly caused by an under-dried electrode or excessive calendaring force.
Stop the line immediately. Reduce calendaring force by 10 kN and re-check. If cracking continues,
notify your Shift Supervisor — the reel may need to be returned for additional drying. Reference: SOP-
ELEC-CAL-004 §5, Step 4.
SOP-ELEC-MIX-001 — Authorised Agratas personnel only. Page

1. Purpose
Defines the operating procedure for slot-die coating of electrode slurry onto aluminium (cathode) and
copper (anode) current collector foil. Covers machine start-up, process parameter setting, in-process
monitoring, and shutdown.
2. Safety Requirements
NMP solvent vapour is present around the coating machine. Drying oven operates up
to 150 °C — risk of burns and solvent inhalation. PPE mandatory: heat-resistant
🦺 SAFETY
gloves near oven exits, chemical-resistant gloves near slurry, safety goggles. Confirm
oven door interlocks functional before start-up.
3. Pre-Coating Checks
28. Verify slurry batch ID and viscosity match coating work order. Slurry age must be < 4 hours. If >
4 hours, re-measure viscosity before proceeding.
29. Check foil reel: correct type (Al 16 µm ±1 µm for cathode; Cu 8 µm ±0.5 µm for anode), no
visible defects (wrinkles, holes, scratches).
30. Inspect slot die: clean lips, no dried slurry. Die gap set per recipe. Die bolts torqued to 8 N·m.
31. Prime slurry pump: run until no air bubbles in return line (minimum 2 minutes).
